text: The surgeon suspends the left lateral segment of the liver by placing a Keith needle with silk suture to the crura and the right side of the falciform ligament.
The surgeon attempts to enter the lesser sac, maneuvering instruments with difficulty due to the patient's small size, despite all ports being placed below the umbilicus.
The surgeon incises the gastrocolic omentum using a vessel sealer, carefully navigating below the right gastroepiploic artery while ensuring the transverse colon remains unharmed.

The surgeon carefully dissects the severe adhesions at the pylorus, where the transverse colon and gallbladder are adhered. The gastrocolic omentum is incised, and the stomach is retracted to improve exposure of the surgical field.

The surgeon retracts the stomach further to enter the lesser sac, revealing severe adhesions between the gallbladder and the stomach. The gallbladder is retracted to identify the duodenum, which is typically positioned beneath the gallbladder.
The surgeon carefully dissects severe adhesions caused by inflammation from a peptic ulcer, avoiding entry into the gallbladder while separating the adhered tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects above the lesser curvature, attempting to identify the right gastric vessels amidst severe inflammation, while being cautious of the deeper common bile duct.
The surgeon uses a robotic system to continue the dissection, applying pressure to control bleeding that occurs during the procedure.

The surgeon examines the surgical field and identifies the splenic vein, noting the patient's abnormal anatomy. The laparoscopic ports are positioned below the umbilicus, but the large and redundant stomach complicates the lower dissection.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and incises the gastrocolic omentum below the right gastroepiploic artery using a bipolar device. The incision is extended distally towards the patient's right to reach the base of the right gastroepiploic pedicle. The transverse colon is visible on the left side of the screen, along with the gastrocolic trunk.
The surgeon incises the attachments between the stomach and pancreas, retracting the stomach to improve exposure. The right gastroepiploic vessels are visible and appear engorged.

The surgeon clips and transects the artery and vein using a vessel sealer. The remaining gastrocolic omentum is dissected, revealing inflammation. The pylorus is visibly adherent to the head of the pancreas.

The surgeon identifies the pancreas above the lesser curve and the splenic vein, which is visible heading towards the hiatus and the tail of the pancreas on the right of the screen. The left and right gastric vessels are identified, and the hepatogastric omentum is incised, revealing the inferior border of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a green load stapler to transect the proximal stomach, ensuring the entire antrum is resected during the distal gastrectomy. Compression is maintained for one minute before completing the transection. The surgeon attempts to separate the pylorus from the head of the pancreas but encounters difficulty, leading to a decision to convert to an open procedure.
